perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall keep a written record of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials furnished to it and the location of such Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. At any time, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all copies of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within five (5) business days of such request
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to maintain a record of the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials provided to it and the general location of such items. At the Disclosing Party's request, reasonable efforts will be made to return copies of the Confidential Information and Confidential Materials to the Disclosing Party within a commercially reasonable timeframe of such request,
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall keep a written record" to "shall endeavor to maintain a record" weakens the obligation to keep a precise record. Replacing "the location of such Confidential Information and Confidential Materials" with "the general location of such items" introduces ambiguity. The strict requirement of returning all copies within five business days is weakened to "reasonable efforts will be made to return copies" within a "commercially reasonable timeframe."
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.003(4) requires those who misappropriate trade secrets to take active steps to protect them. By weakening the requirement to keep accurate records and making the return of the confidential information and materials less stringent, the modified text reduces the Receiving Party's responsibility for safeguarding trade secrets, potentially facilitating misappropriation, and contradicting the spirit and intent of the law.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intent of Florida Statute 688.003 by weakening the Receiving Party's responsibility to protect trade secrets. The original text mandates a 'written record' of confidential information and materials, and the return of 'all copies' within five business days. The changed text weakens these obligations, requiring only an endeavor to maintain a record, specifying the 'general location' of items, and calling for 'reasonable efforts' to return copies within a 'commercially reasonable timeframe.' This directly reduces the Receiving Party's accountability, potentially facilitating misappropriation, which Florida Statute 688.003 aims to prevent. The law snippet 1 mentions that 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order' implying that the Receiving Party has an obligation to protect the trade secret, this change weakens that obligation. The second snippet is unusable.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Disclosing Party is not waiving, and shall not be deemed to have waived, its attorney-client privilege with respect to any Confidential Informa tion or Confidential Materials (including, without limitation, Confidential Information or Confidential Materials pertaining to pending or threatened litigation) which may be disclosed hereunder by the Disclo sing Party and with respect to which the Disclosing Party has asserted, or is entitled to assert, its attorney-client privilege.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that the Disclosing Party may not be waiving, and should not be automatically deemed to have waived, its attorney-client privilege. This applies to Confidential Information or Confidential Materials, including those relating to pending or threatened litigation, where the Disclosing Party has asserted, or could potentially assert, its attorney-client privilege.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text uses conditional language ('may not be waiving', 'should not be automatically deemed') instead of definitive statements ('is not waiving', 'shall not be deemed'). Replacing 'is entitled to assert' with 'could potentially assert' introduces uncertainty regarding the Disclosing Party's actual privilege status.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 90.502 defines the attorney-client privilege and its scope. By introducing uncertainty regarding whether the privilege is being waived, the revised text undermines the clear protection afforded by the statute, potentially leading to disputes about whether specific communications are indeed privileged and shielded from disclosure. The statute intends a clear framework to avoid ambiguity and the modification obfuscates it.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title VII > Chapter 90 > Section 502 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title VII EVIDENCE Chapter 90 EVIDENCE CODE Entire Chapter SECTION 502 Lawyer-client privilege. F.S. 90.502 90.502 Lawyer-client privilege. — (1) For purposes of this section: (a) A “lawyer” is a person authorized, or reasonably believed by the client to be authorized, to practice law in any state or nation. (b) A “client” is any person, public officer, corporation, association, or other organization or entity, either public or private, who consults a lawyer with the purpose of obtaining legal services or who is rendered legal services by a lawyer. (c) A communication between lawyer and client is “confidential” if it is not intended to be disclosed to third persons other than: 1. Those to whom disclosure is in furtherance of the rendition of legal services to the client. 2. Those reasonably necessary for the transmission of the communication. (2) A client has a privilege to refuse to disclose, and to prevent any other person from disclosing, the contents of confidential communications when such other person learned of the communications because they were made in the rendition of legal services to the client. (3) The privilege may be claimed by: (a) The client. (b) A guardian or conservator of the client. (c) The personal representative of a deceased client. (d) A successor, assignee, trustee in dissolution, or any similar representative of an organization, corporation, or association or other entity, either public or private, whether or not in existence. (e) The lawyer, but only on behalf of the client. The lawyer’s authority to claim the privilege is presumed in the absence of contrary evidence. (4) There is no lawyer-client privilege under this section when: (a) The services of the lawyer were sought or obtained to enable or aid anyone to commit or plan to commit what the client knew was a crime or fraud. (b) A communication is relevant to an issue between parties who claim through the same deceased client. (c) A communication is relevant to an issue of breach of duty by the lawyer to the client or by the client to the lawyer, arising from the lawyer-client relationshi
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures the attorney-client privilege is not waived. The changed text introduces uncertainty by stating the Disclosing Party 'may not be waiving' the privilege and that they 'should not be automatically deemed' to have waived it, and replaces 'is entitled to assert' with 'could potentially assert'. According to the Law Explanation, this undermines the clear protection afforded by Florida Statute 90.502, potentially leading to disputes about whether specific communications are privileged. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Florida Statute 90.502 regarding lawyer-client privilege, reinforcing the importance of clarity in this area. The change introduces ambiguity where clarity is legally expected, creating a contradiction. The perturbation explanation highlights the shift from definitive statements to conditional language, contributing to the ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from any damages, losses, costs, or lia bilities arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Pa rty or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Ma terials or other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, including, but not limited t o, reasonable attorneys’ fees, court costs and other litigation expenses incurred in connection with the enforcem ent of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may be responsible for indemnifying the Disclosing Party from certain damages, losses, costs, or liabilities arguably arising out of or potentially resulting from unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Party or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials or a possible breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, potentially including reasonable attorneys’ fees, court costs, and other litigation expenses related to the enforcement of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall indemnify and hold harmless" to "may be responsible for indemnifying" introduces uncertainty about the indemnification obligation. Replacing "arising out of or resulting from" with "arguably arising out of or potentially resulting from" and "other breach" with "a possible breach" further weakens the causal link and the certainty of a breach. Replacing 'including' with 'potentially including' weakens the attorneys' fees, court costs, etc. obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a specific statute on indemnification clauses, Florida contract law requires clear and unambiguous language in indemnification agreements. The modified text's uncertainty and conditional language undermines the clear allocation of risk and liability, making the indemnification clause difficult to enforce and potentially unenforceable under Florida contract law principles that require clarity and certainty in contractual obligations. This may be difficult to show a violation, but given Florida Statute 82.021 establishes the Florida Constitution as the highest law in Florida and that it guides interpretation of contracts, there is an ambiguous condition as Florida's court system requires clearly written contracts.

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall indemnify and hold harmless" to "may be responsible for indemnifying" introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's obligation. The Florida Statute snippet 1 shows the Florida Statutes are updated annually, implying the need for contracts to remain current and clear. The modification significantly weakens the enforceability of the indemnification clause because 'may' introduces uncertainty, which contradicts the need for clarity in contractual obligations as stated in the Law Explanation. The change from 'arising out of or resulting from' to 'arguably arising out of or potentially resulting from' further weakens the causal link necessary for indemnification.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties irrevocably and uncond itionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Florida and of the Un ited States of America located in the State of Florida, County of Hillsborough, for any ac tions, suits or proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may consent to the jurisdiction of courts in Florida, specifically Hillsborough County, for actions related to this Agreement, although other jurisdictions might also be considered appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "irrevocably and unconditionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction" to "may consent to the jurisdiction...although other jurisdictions might also be considered appropriate" makes jurisdiction uncertain. It weakens the agreement to be bound to Florida courts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Law on Forum Selection Clauses
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida courts generally enforce forum selection clauses unless they are shown to be unreasonable. The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the agreement on jurisdiction, creating a situation where the clause may be deemed unenforceable due to the lack of clear and unequivocal consent, contrary to the principles established in *Manrique v. Fausnight* and similar cases that emphasize the importance of certainty in forum selection.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies an 'exclusive jurisdiction' in Florida, creating a clear and enforceable agreement. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the parties 'may consent' to Florida jurisdiction and that 'other jurisdictions might also be considered appropriate.' This weakens the binding nature of the jurisdiction clause, potentially making it unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this shift from a definitive agreement to an uncertain one, which could be viewed as non-compliant, falling under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. Although no specific law snippets are provided, the Law Explanation states that Florida courts generally enforce forum selection clauses unless unreasonable and emphasizes the importance of certainty, which this modification undermines.

perturbation.original_text: No publicity, release or announcem ent concerning this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby shall be made by either Party without advance written approval thereof by the other Part y.
perturbation.changed_text: Parties should generally avoid public announcements about this Agreement without first consulting the other Party, but reasonable exceptions may apply.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be made by either Party without advance written approval' with 'should generally avoid public announcements about this Agreement without first consulting the other Party, but reasonable exceptions may apply' creates an ambiguity. Now the clause is discretionary. What is 'reasonable?'
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Burger King Corp. v. Weaver, 169 F.3d 1310 (11th Cir. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. While this doesn't prevent parties from exercising their contractual rights, it does prevent them from doing so in a way that unfairly frustrates the other party's reasonable expectations. By introducing ambiguity with 'reasonable exceptions,' the modified clause creates a potential for a party to circumvent the publicity restriction in bad faith, arguing that their actions fall within a 'reasonable exception,' even if it harms the Disclosing Party, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated advance written approval for any publicity, ensuring control over information release. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating parties 'should generally avoid' public announcements, but allows for 'reasonable exceptions.' This directly contradicts the original clear requirement for advance approval, creating a loophole that could be exploited. The 'reasonable exceptions' clause undermines the original intent of preventing unauthorized publicity, making enforcement uncertain. The Law Explanation correctly points out the potential for bad faith circumvention due to the ambiguity, which makes this Ambiguity fall under the Legal Contradiction definition.
